narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40 and an LSAT score of 35.50, you've clearly put in the effort to build a strong academic foundation, coming from a lower-middle income family and planning to attend law school full-time. The admissions model we use predicted a 56.3% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which tipped the scales just enough to result in the rejection of your application—our goal is to select students with the highest likelihood of passing to ensure their success. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, which is notably lower than the typical student's 5th or 6th decile, signaling to the model a potential dip in performance during those crucial later undergraduate years. This outweighed the positive influences from your LSAT score, which is close to the average of 36.70 and pushed the model toward expecting you to pass, as well as your above-average undergraduate GPA of 3.40 compared to the typical 3.21. Your Year 1 decile of 2nd also nudged things slightly toward failure, being well below the average 5th or 6th decile, while your family income bracket and full-time enrollment status offered minor boosts in the model's eyes, aligning with common profiles among successful bar takers. Overall, your profile stands out as having stronger test scores and GPA than many applicants, but the lower decile rankings in school performance raised red flags when stacked against peers who maintained higher consistent standings. I know this news is disappointing, especially given your dedication, and it's okay to feel frustrated—many strong candidates face this hurdle. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your later-year decile rankings, perhaps through post-baccalaureate options or targeted GPA improvement, and reapply once you've strengthened those areas. You might also explore schools with more holistic reviews or gather letters emphasizing your resilience from a lower-middle income background. We're rooting for you to turn this around and succeed in law—reach out to admissions counselors for personalized advice on building an even stronger case next time. Keep pushing; your potential is evident.
